name: Hydrogen
facts: Most abundant element in the universe
Makes up ~75% of all baryonic mass
Only element with no neutrons in its most common isotope

name: Helium
facts: Second most abundant element in the observable universe
Lowest boiling point of all elements
Cannot be solidified at normal pressure

name: Lithium
facts: Lightest metal on the periodic table
Floats on water
Used in mood-stabilizing drugs

name: Beryllium
facts: Extremely light and stiff
Toxic when inhaled as dust
Transparent to X-rays

name: Boron
discoveredBy: Joseph Louis Gay-Lussac, Louis Jacques Thénard
facts: Essential micronutrient for plants
Harder than most metals
Used in nuclear reactor control rods

name: Carbon
facts: Basis of all known life
Forms more compounds than any other element
Diamond is the hardest natural substance

name: Nitrogen
facts: Makes up 78% of Earth's atmosphere
Liquid nitrogen is used in cryotherapy
Triple bond in N₂ is extremely strong

name: Oxygen
facts: Third most abundant element in the universe
Makes up 21% of Earth's atmosphere
Liquid oxygen is pale blue

name: Fluorine
facts: Most electronegative element
Most reactive nonmetal
Attacks glass

name: Neon
facts: Third most abundant noble gas in Earth's atmosphere
Neon lights are actually red-orange, not all colors
Discovered the same year as krypton and xenon

name: Sodium
facts: Reacts violently with water
Essential for nerve function
Burns yellow in flame tests

name: Magnesium
facts: Burns with an intensely bright white flame
Essential for photosynthesis
Lightest structural metal

name: Aluminum
facts: Most abundant metal in Earth's crust
Was once more valuable than gold
Fully recyclable without loss of quality

name: Silicon
facts: Second most abundant element in Earth's crust
Foundation of modern electronics
Sand is mostly silicon dioxide

name: Phosphorus
facts: First element discovered by a known person
Essential for all life
White phosphorus glows in the dark

name: Sulfur
facts: Known as 'brimstone' in the Bible
Essential for amino acids cysteine and methionine
Smells like rotten eggs when burned

name: Chlorine
facts: Used as a chemical weapon in WWI
Essential for digestion (stomach acid)
Greenish-yellow toxic gas

name: Argon
facts: Third most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere
Name means 'lazy' in Greek
Most commonly used noble gas

name: Potassium
facts: Essential for heart function
Bananas are a good dietary source
Symbol K comes from 'kalium'

name: Calcium
facts: Most abundant metal in the human body
Essential for muscle contraction
Fifth most abundant element in Earth's crust

name: Scandium
facts: First transition metal
Its existence was predicted by Mendeleev
Very rare in Earth's crust

name: Titanium
facts: Highest strength-to-weight ratio of any metal
Biocompatible with human tissue
Named after the Titans of Greek mythology

name: Vanadium
facts: Named after the Scandinavian goddess Vanadis (Freyja)
Found in some crude oils
Hardens steel significantly

name: Chromium
facts: Name means 'color' in Greek due to colorful compounds
Makes rubies red and emeralds green
Half-filled d-shell gives unusual electron config

name: Manganese
facts: Essential trace element in humans
Found in deep ocean nodules
Ancient cave paintings used manganese pigments

name: Iron
facts: Most abundant element by mass in Earth's core
Makes blood red
Most used metal in the world

name: Cobalt
facts: Used for blue coloring in glass for thousands of years
Radioactive Co-60 used in cancer treatment
Name comes from German 'Kobold' (goblin)

name: Nickel
facts: Main component of the Earth's core
Named after a mischievous spirit 'Nickel'
Most US nickels are only 25% nickel

name: Copper
facts: One of the first metals used by humans
Naturally antimicrobial
Statue of Liberty is coated in copper (green patina)

name: Zinc
facts: Essential trace element for humans
Fourth most common metal in use
Pennies (post-1982) are mostly zinc

name: Gallium
facts: Melts in your hand (mp 30°C)
Predicted by Mendeleev as 'eka-aluminum'
Wets glass, forming a mirror

name: Germanium
facts: Predicted by Mendeleev as 'eka-silicon'
Discovery confirmed Mendeleev's periodic law
Used in early transistors

name: Arsenic
facts: Famous as 'inheritance powder' poison
Used in LEDs and laser diodes
Sublimes directly from solid to gas

name: Selenium
discoveredBy: Jöns Jacob Berzelius, Johann Gottlieb Gahn
facts: Named after the Moon (Selene)
Essential micronutrient
Conducts electricity when exposed to light

name: Bromine
discoveredBy: Antoine Jérôme Balard, Carl Jacob Löwig
facts: One of only two elements liquid at room temperature
Name means 'stench' in Greek
Reddish-brown fuming liquid

name: Krypton
facts: Name means 'hidden' in Greek
Former definition of meter used krypton wavelength
Can form some compounds unlike lighter noble gases

name: Rubidium
facts: First element discovered by spectroscopy
Name means 'deep red' in Latin
Spontaneously ignites in air

name: Strontium
facts: Named after Strontian, Scotland
Sr-90 is a dangerous radioactive fallout product
Burns brilliant red in flame tests

name: Yttrium
facts: Named after Ytterby, Sweden (which gave names to 4 elements)
Used to make the red color in TVs
Helps make superconductors work at higher temperatures

name: Zirconium
facts: Almost completely transparent to neutrons
Cubic zirconia is used as a diamond substitute
Highly corrosion resistant

name: Niobium
facts: Originally named Columbium in the US
Becomes superconducting at 9.26 K
Used in Large Hadron Collider magnets

name: Molybdenum
facts: Essential trace element in all organisms
Has the sixth highest melting point
Used in nitrogen fixation by bacteria

name: Technetium
facts: First artificially produced element
All isotopes are radioactive
Tc-99m is the most widely used medical radioisotope

name: Ruthenium
facts: Named after Russia (Ruthenia)
Can be used to make solar cells more efficient
Platinum group metal

name: Rhodium
facts: One of the rarest and most expensive elements
Catalytic converters use 0.2-0.5g of rhodium
Named after Greek 'rhodon' (rose) for its rose-colored compounds

name: Palladium
facts: Can absorb 900 times its volume in hydrogen
Named after asteroid Pallas
Significant price increases due to catalytic converter demand

name: Silver
facts: Highest electrical conductivity of all elements
Highest thermal conductivity of all metals
Symbol Ag from Latin 'argentum'

name: Cadmium
discoveredBy: Karl Samuel Leberecht Hermann, Friedrich Stromeyer
facts: Highly toxic, major environmental pollutant
Its compounds produce vivid yellow, orange, and red pigments
Used in quantum dot technology

name: Indium
facts: Critical material for smartphones and tablets
Makes a 'tin cry' sound when bent
Named after indigo spectral line

name: Tin
facts: Known to ancient civilizations
The 'tin cry' (transformation at ~13°C) caused Napoleon's army problems
Symbol Sn from Latin 'stannum'

name: Antimony
facts: Used as eye makeup in ancient Egypt
Symbol Sb from Latin 'stibium'
One of only a few elements that expand when freezing

name: Tellurium
facts: One of the rarest stable elements
Named after Earth (Tellus)
Exposure causes garlic breath for weeks

name: Iodine
facts: Essential for thyroid function
Sublimes directly from solid to purple vapor
Only halogen solid at room temperature

name: Xenon
facts: Name means 'stranger' in Greek
First noble gas to form a true chemical compound (XePtF₆)
Used in ion thrusters for spacecraft

name: Cesium
facts: Most electropositive stable element
Melts in your hand (mp 28°C)
Cesium-133 defines the SI second

name: Barium
facts: Name means 'heavy' in Greek
Barium sulfate is used for GI tract X-rays
Burns green in flame tests

name: Lanthanum
facts: First of the lanthanide series
Name means 'to lie hidden'
Used in nickel-metal hydride batteries

name: Cerium
discoveredBy: Martin Heinrich Klaproth, Jöns Jacob Berzelius, Wilhelm Hisinger
facts: Most abundant rare earth element
Named after asteroid Ceres
Pyrophoric when scratched

name: Praseodymium
facts: Name means 'green twin'
Used to make aircraft engines
Makes brilliant yellow-green glass

name: Neodymium
facts: Nd₂Fe₁₄B magnets are the strongest permanent magnets
Critical for green energy technology
Name means 'new twin'

name: Promethium
discoveredBy: Jacob A. Marinsky, Lawrence E. Glendenin, Charles D. Coryell
facts: Only lanthanide with no stable isotopes
Named after Prometheus from Greek mythology
Extremely rare in Earth's crust (about 500g total)

name: Samarium
facts: Named after Samarskite mineral (named after a Russian mine official)
SmCo magnets retain magnetism at high temperatures
One of the few elements with even number of stable isotopes (7)

name: Europium
facts: Named after Europe
Used in anti-counterfeiting measures for Euro notes
Least dense and most reactive lanthanide

name: Gadolinium
facts: Highest neutron absorption cross-section of stable elements
MRI contrast dye gadolinium enhances images
Named after Johan Gadolin (Finnish chemist)

name: Terbium
facts: One of four elements named after Ytterby, Sweden
Terfenol-D changes shape in magnetic field
Bright green emission used in tricolor fluorescent lights

name: Dysprosium
facts: Name means 'hard to obtain' in Greek
Critical for high-temperature neodymium magnets in EVs
Has highest magnetic strength per atom at low temperatures

name: Holmium
facts: Highest magnetic moment of any element
Named after Stockholm (Holmia)
Ho:YAG lasers used in urological surgery

name: Erbium
facts: Named after Ytterby, Sweden
Critical for long-distance fiber optic communications
Er:YAG lasers used in dentistry and skin treatments

name: Thulium
facts: Rarest naturally occurring lanthanide
Named after Thule (mythical northern region)
Tm-170 used in portable medical X-ray devices

name: Ytterbium
facts: Named after Ytterby, Sweden (4th element from there)
Used in most precise optical atomic clocks
Full 4f shell gives unusual properties

name: Lutetium
discoveredBy: Carl Auer von Welsbach, Georges Urbain
facts: Last and hardest lanthanide
Named after Lutetia (ancient Paris)
Lu-177 is used in targeted cancer radiotherapy

name: Hafnium
facts: Named after Hafnia (Latin for Copenhagen)
Last stable element to be discovered
Chemically nearly identical to zirconium

name: Tantalum
facts: Named after King Tantalus of Greek mythology
Highly biocompatible
Found in coltan ore from conflict zones

name: Tungsten
facts: Highest melting point of all elements (3422°C)
Highest boiling point of all elements
Symbol W from 'Wolfram'

name: Rhenium
discoveredBy: Masataka Ogawa, Walter Noddack, Ida Tacke
facts: Last stable element to be discovered in nature
One of the densest elements
Named after the Rhine River

name: Osmium
facts: Densest naturally occurring element (22.59 g/cm³)
OsO₄ used to stain biological tissues
Named after Greek 'osme' (smell) due to pungent oxide

name: Iridium
facts: Second densest element
K-Pg extinction boundary shows iridium spike from asteroid
Most corrosion-resistant metal known

name: Platinum
facts: Name comes from Spanish 'platina' (little silver)
Used in the first definition of the kilogram
Major catalyst in petroleum refining

name: Gold
facts: Only metal that is yellow in color
Relativistic effects cause its golden color
All gold ever mined would fill ~3.5 Olympic swimming pools

name: Mercury
facts: Only metal liquid at room temperature
Named after the planet Mercury
Symbol Hg from 'hydrargyrum' (liquid silver)

name: Thallium
facts: Named after green spectral line (Greek 'thallos' = green shoot)
Notorious poison historically
Tl-201 used in heart imaging

name: Lead
facts: Heaviest stable element
Most stable end product of radioactive decay chains
Romans used lead pipes (plumbum → plumbing)

name: Bismuth
facts: Heaviest stable primordial element (Bi-209 has half-life >10^19 years)
Creates beautiful iridescent oxide crystals
Least toxic heavy metal

name: Polonium
facts: Named after Marie Curie's homeland, Poland
First element discovered by Curie
Used to assassinate Alexander Litvinenko in 2006

name: Astatine
discoveredBy: Dale R. Corson, Kenneth Ross MacKenzie, Emilio Segrè
facts: Rarest naturally occurring element on Earth
At any given time, less than 1 gram exists in Earth's crust
At-211 used in alpha-particle therapy for cancer

name: Radon
facts: All isotopes radioactive
Second leading cause of lung cancer (from soil)
Named after radium (its decay product)

name: Francium
facts: Most unstable naturally occurring element
Only about 20-30 atoms may exist in Earth's crust at any time
Named after France, discovered by a French scientist

name: Radium
facts: Discovered by Marie and Pierre Curie
Once used in quack health products
Word 'radioactivity' coined by Marie Curie

name: Actinium
facts: Gives its name to the actinide series
Glows faint blue in the dark due to radiation
100 times more radioactive than radium

name: Thorium
facts: Named after Thor, Norse god of thunder
Potential nuclear fuel more abundant than uranium
Thorium reactors produce less waste than uranium reactors

name: Protactinium
facts: Name means 'before actinium'
One of the rarest and most expensive natural elements
Pa-231 has half-life of 32,760 years

name: Uranium
facts: Heaviest naturally occurring element
U-235 is fissile (used in nuclear reactions)
Named after the planet Uranus

name: Neptunium
facts: First transuranic element discovered
Named after the planet Neptune
Found in trace amounts in uranium ores

name: Plutonium
discoveredBy: Glenn T. Seaborg, Arthur Wahl, Joseph W. Kennedy, Edwin McMillan
facts: Used in Trinity and Fat Man atomic bombs
Pu-238 powered Voyager and Cassini spacecraft
Named after Pluto (then a planet)

name: Americium
discoveredBy: Glenn T. Seaborg, Ralph A. James, Leon O. Morgan, Albert Ghiorso
facts: In virtually every smoke detector
Named after the Americas
Am-241 emits alpha particles used in smoke detectors

name: Curium
discoveredBy: Glenn T. Seaborg, Ralph A. James, Albert Ghiorso
facts: Named after Marie and Pierre Curie
Used in Alpha Particle X-ray Spectrometer on Mars rovers
Glows red from its own radioactive heat

name: Berkelium
discoveredBy: Glenn T. Seaborg, Stanley G. Thompson, Albert Ghiorso
facts: Named after Berkeley, California
Used to synthesize element 117 (tennessine)
Less than 1 gram produced since discovery

name: Californium
discoveredBy: Glenn T. Seaborg, Stanley G. Thompson, Albert Ghiorso, Kenneth Street Jr.
uses: Neutron startup source for nuclear reactors
facts: Named after California and University of California
Most neutron-emitting isotope known
1 microgram can cost $27 million

name: Einsteinium
facts: Discovered in fallout from first hydrogen bomb test
Named after Albert Einstein
Discovery was classified until 1955

name: Fermium
facts: Also discovered in Ivy Mike hydrogen bomb fallout
Named after Enrico Fermi
Heaviest element that can be produced by neutron bombardment

name: Mendelevium
facts: Named after Dmitri Mendeleev
First element created one atom at a time
Only 17 atoms were created in the first synthesis

name: Nobelium
discoveredBy: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research (Dubna, Russia)
facts: Named after Alfred Nobel
Discovery was controversial between US and Soviet teams
Nobelium-259 has half-life of 58 minutes

name: Lawrencium
facts: Named after Ernest Lawrence (inventor of the cyclotron)
Last actinide element
Has anomalous electron configuration due to relativistic effects

name: Rutherfordium
discoveredBy: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
facts: Named after Ernest Rutherford
First transactinide element
Discovery disputed between JINR and LBNL

name: Dubnium
facts: Named after Dubna, Russia
Half-life of most stable isotope is 32 hours
Discovery claimed simultaneously by US and Soviet scientists

name: Seaborgium
facts: Named after Glenn Seaborg (still alive at the time — controversial)
Seaborg was first person to have an element named after them while living
Most stable isotope has half-life of ~2.1 minutes

name: Bohrium
facts: Named after Niels Bohr
Most stable isotope Bh-270 has half-life of ~61 seconds
Synthesized by bombarding bismuth with chromium nuclei

name: Hassium
facts: Named after Hesse, Germany
OsO₄ analog HsO₄ has been synthesized
Most stable known isotope is Hs-270 (half-life ~22 seconds)

name: Meitnerium
facts: Named after Lise Meitner (co-discoverer of nuclear fission)
Half-life of most stable isotope Mt-278 is ~7.6 seconds
Properties predicted by extrapolation from lighter group 9 elements

name: Darmstadtium
facts: Named after Darmstadt, Germany
Only 11 atoms have ever been observed
Half-life of most stable isotope is about 12.7 seconds

name: Roentgenium
facts: Named after Wilhelm Röntgen (discoverer of X-rays)
Temporary name was unununium (Uuu)
Relativistic effects predicted to make it look like gold

name: Copernicium
facts: Named after Nicolaus Copernicus
Predicted to behave as a noble gas due to relativistic effects
May be a gas at room temperature

name: Nihonium
facts: Named after Japan (Nihon in Japanese)
First element discovered in Asia
Half-life of most stable isotope is ~20 seconds

name: Flerovium
discoveredBy: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research (JINR), Dubna
facts: Named after Flerov Laboratory of Nuclear Reactions
Predicted to behave as a noble gas due to relativistic effects
May be an island of stability candidate

name: Moscovium
discoveredBy: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
facts: Named after Moscow Oblast, Russia
Confirmed as a new element in 2015
Half-life of Mc-290 is about 650 milliseconds

name: Livermorium
discoveredBy: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
facts: Named after Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
Most stable isotope Lv-293 has half-life of 60 milliseconds
Predicted to have some metallic properties

name: Tennessine
discoveredBy: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Vanderbilt University
facts: Named after Tennessee, USA
Second heaviest confirmed element
Predicted to have metallic properties unlike lighter halogens

name: Oganesson
discoveredBy: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
facts: Heaviest confirmed element
Predicted to be a solid at room temperature unlike lighter noble gases
Named after Yuri Oganessian (still living)
